content.problemStatement:
You are given a **sorted array of integers** AAA of size NNN. In this array, **every element occurs exactly twice**, except for **one element which occurs only once**.

Find and print the element that occurs **only once**.

Your solution should run in O(logN)O(log N)O(logN) time and use O(1)O(1)O(1) extra memory.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindSinglefindSinglefindSingle – This function finds the unique element that appears only once in a sorted array.

Parameters
numsnumsnums : A sorted array of integers where every element appears exactly twice except one.
Return Value
Returns a single integer — the element that occurs only once.
Constraints
1≤N≤1051 \leq N \leq 10^51≤N≤105
0≤nums[i]≤1050 \leq nums[i] \leq 10^50≤nums[i]≤105
Array is sorted in non-decreasing order
Exactly **one** element appears once; all others appear exactly twice

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers representing the array elements.


Output Format

Print a single integer — the element that occurs only once.
constraints: Array is sorted in non‑decreasing order
Exactly one element appears once; all others appear exactly twice
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an odd integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 100000) — the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers in non‑decreasing order. Exactly one element appears once; all other elements appear exactly twice.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the integer that occurs only once.

sampleTestCases.explanation: In the first test case the only element without a pair is 5. In the second test case the unpaired element is 8.
Array of size one, the single element is trivially the answer.
The unique element is at the beginning of the array.

solution.approach:
Because the array is sorted and every element except one appears twice, the pairs are adjacent. If we look at an even index i, the element at i should be equal to the element at i+1 unless the unique element lies at or before i. By performing a binary search on the index range and always moving to the half that still contains the mismatch, we locate the single element in O(log N) time. The algorithm:
1. Initialise left = 0, right = N‑1.
2. While left < right:
   a. mid = (left + right) / 2 (integer division).
   b. If mid is odd, decrement it to make it even.
   c. If nums[mid] == nums[mid+1], the unique element is after this pair → left = mid + 2.
   d. Else, the unique element is at mid or before → right = mid.
3. When the loop ends, left == right and points to the unique element.
The method uses only constant extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log N) per test case, because each iteration halves the search interval.
